mainmatter.p: One of the most common tasks when working in univalent foundations is to characterize the path space of a given type, i.e. establish a structure identity principle. Formalized SIP proofs often follow a baroque style involving identity systems and the even more complex displayed identity systems, etc. that sometimes obscure the very simple informal reasoning behind a given structure identity principle; I would like to develop a suite of lemmas and tactics that better reflect this informal reasoning.
To understand what I mean, let us consider how we might characterize the path spaces of morphisms in a co-slice A \downarrow \mathcal{U}.

Let A be a type; an A-algebra X is defined to be a type { \left \lvert X \right \rvert } equipped with a function { A } \xrightarrow{{ \alpha _A }} { { \left \lvert X \right \rvert } }. A homomorphism { X } \xrightarrow{{ f }} { Y } of A-algebras is defined to be a function { { \left \lvert X \right \rvert } } \xrightarrow{{ { \left \lvert f \right \rvert } }} { { \left \lvert Y \right \rvert } } together with a homotopy \alpha _f \colon { \left \lvert f \right \rvert } \circ \alpha _X \sim \alpha _Y. A homotopy H \colon f \sim g between homomorphisms { X } \xrightarrow{{ f,g }} { Y } is given by a homotopy { \left \lvert H \right \rvert } \colon { \left \lvert f \right \rvert } \sim { \left \lvert g \right \rvert } together with an assignment to each a:A of an identification \alpha _H a \colon { \left \lvert H \right \rvert } \alpha _X a \cdot \eta _g a = \eta _f a.

frontmatter.title: Structure identity principle for co-slice morphisms

mainmatter.p: Let A be a type and let { X } \xrightarrow{{ f }} { Y } be a homomorphism of A-algebras. Assuming function extensionality, the type { \textstyle \sum _{ g \colon X \to Y }} g \sim f of homomorphisms homotopic to f is contractible.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: We expand the dependent sum { \textstyle \sum _{ g \colon X \to Y }} g \sim f as a flat record telescope:
block.tex: \begin{bmatrix*} [l] { \left \lvert g \right \rvert } & \colon & { \left \lvert X \right \rvert } \to { \left \lvert Y \right \rvert } \\ \alpha _g & \colon & { \left \lvert g \right \rvert } \circ \alpha _X \sim \alpha _Y \\ { \left \lvert H \right \rvert } & \colon & { \left \lvert f \right \rvert } \sim { \left \lvert g \right \rvert } \\ \alpha _H & \colon & { \textstyle \prod _{ a:A }} { \left \lvert H \right \rvert } \alpha _X a \cdot \alpha _g a = \alpha _f a \end{bmatrix*}

\begin{bmatrix*} [l] { \left \lvert g \right \rvert } & \colon & { \left \lvert X \right \rvert } \to { \left \lvert Y \right \rvert } \\ { \left \lvert H \right \rvert } & \colon & { \left \lvert f \right \rvert } \sim { \left \lvert g \right \rvert } \\ \alpha _g & \colon & { \left \lvert g \right \rvert } \circ \alpha _X \sim \alpha _Y \\ \alpha _H & \colon & { \textstyle \prod _{ a:A }} { \left \lvert H \right \rvert } \alpha _X a \cdot \alpha _g a = \alpha _f a \end{bmatrix*}
block.p: By homotopy induction (function extensionality), the first two fields contract to a point with substitution { \left [ { \left \lvert g \right \rvert } : \equiv { \left \lvert f \right \rvert } ; { \left \lvert H \right \rvert } : \equiv \lambda \_ . \mathsf{refl} \right ]}. What remains of our telescope is the following:
Zooming into the \alpha _H component, we simplify it and then zoom back out:

By dependent homotopy induction (also function extensionality), the remaining data collapse to a point with substitution { \left [ \alpha _g : \equiv \alpha _f; \alpha _H : \equiv \lambda \_ . \mathsf{refl} \right ]}. The remaining telescope is empty, so we are done.

mainmatter.p: The steps performed in the proof of the SIP lemma for co-slice morphisms above are most naturally expressed at the level of a flat telescope (a context). The goal is always to contract a telescope to a point, and each step is either a rearrangement of the telescope, a “zooming in” step that adjusts a component of the telescope, or an application of some form of identity induction to kill off part of the telescope — path induction, homotopy induction, equivalence induction, etc. In general, we want to be able to easily point out the fragment of a telescope that contracts to a point, and then use the resulting substitution to modify the telescope and proceed further until the entire telescope is gone.

I believe that there is a reasonable and practical formal logic for doing such things, and that this logic can be implemented as a series of lemmas in homotopy type theory that can then be animated via tactics for interactive backward-inference proofs of contractibility.
On this page, I accumulate ideas that might be suitable for students at Masters or Bachelor level to try their hand at.

One of the most common tasks when working in univalent foundations is to characterize the path space of a given type, i.e. establish a structure identity principle. Formalized SIP proofs often follow a baroque style involving identity systems and the even more complex displayed identity systems, etc. that sometimes obscure the very simple informal reasoning behind a given structure identity principle; I would like to develop a suite of lemmas and tactics that better reflect this informal reasoning.
To understand what I mean, let us consider how we might characterize the path spaces of morphisms in a co-slice A \downarrow \mathcal{U}.

Let A be a type; an A-algebra X is defined to be a type { \left \lvert X \right \rvert } equipped with a function { A } \xrightarrow{{ \alpha _A }} { { \left \lvert X \right \rvert } }. A homomorphism { X } \xrightarrow{{ f }} { Y } of A-algebras is defined to be a function { { \left \lvert X \right \rvert } } \xrightarrow{{ { \left \lvert f \right \rvert } }} { { \left \lvert Y \right \rvert } } together with a homotopy \alpha _f \colon { \left \lvert f \right \rvert } \circ \alpha _X \sim \alpha _Y. A homotopy H \colon f \sim g between homomorphisms { X } \xrightarrow{{ f,g }} { Y } is given by a homotopy { \left \lvert H \right \rvert } \colon { \left \lvert f \right \rvert } \sim { \left \lvert g \right \rvert } together with an assignment to each a:A of an identification \alpha _H a \colon { \left \lvert H \right \rvert } \alpha _X a \cdot \eta _g a = \eta _f a.

frontmatter.title: Structure identity principle for co-slice morphisms

mainmatter.p: Let A be a type and let { X } \xrightarrow{{ f }} { Y } be a homomorphism of A-algebras. Assuming function extensionality, the type { \textstyle \sum _{ g \colon X \to Y }} g \sim f of homomorphisms homotopic to f is contractible.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: We expand the dependent sum { \textstyle \sum _{ g \colon X \to Y }} g \sim f as a flat record telescope:
block.tex: \begin{bmatrix*} [l] { \left \lvert g \right \rvert } & \colon & { \left \lvert X \right \rvert } \to { \left \lvert Y \right \rvert } \\ \alpha _g & \colon & { \left \lvert g \right \rvert } \circ \alpha _X \sim \alpha _Y \\ { \left \lvert H \right \rvert } & \colon & { \left \lvert f \right \rvert } \sim { \left \lvert g \right \rvert } \\ \alpha _H & \colon & { \textstyle \prod _{ a:A }} { \left \lvert H \right \rvert } \alpha _X a \cdot \alpha _g a = \alpha _f a \end{bmatrix*}

\begin{bmatrix*} [l] { \left \lvert g \right \rvert } & \colon & { \left \lvert X \right \rvert } \to { \left \lvert Y \right \rvert } \\ { \left \lvert H \right \rvert } & \colon & { \left \lvert f \right \rvert } \sim { \left \lvert g \right \rvert } \\ \alpha _g & \colon & { \left \lvert g \right \rvert } \circ \alpha _X \sim \alpha _Y \\ \alpha _H & \colon & { \textstyle \prod _{ a:A }} { \left \lvert H \right \rvert } \alpha _X a \cdot \alpha _g a = \alpha _f a \end{bmatrix*}
block.p: By homotopy induction (function extensionality), the first two fields contract to a point with substitution { \left [ { \left \lvert g \right \rvert } : \equiv { \left \lvert f \right \rvert } ; { \left \lvert H \right \rvert } : \equiv \lambda \_ . \mathsf{refl} \right ]}. What remains of our telescope is the following:
Zooming into the \alpha _H component, we simplify it and then zoom back out:

By dependent homotopy induction (also function extensionality), the remaining data collapse to a point with substitution { \left [ \alpha _g : \equiv \alpha _f; \alpha _H : \equiv \lambda \_ . \mathsf{refl} \right ]}. The remaining telescope is empty, so we are done.

mainmatter.p: The steps performed in the proof of the SIP lemma for co-slice morphisms above are most naturally expressed at the level of a flat telescope (a context). The goal is always to contract a telescope to a point, and each step is either a rearrangement of the telescope, a “zooming in” step that adjusts a component of the telescope, or an application of some form of identity induction to kill off part of the telescope — path induction, homotopy induction, equivalence induction, etc. In general, we want to be able to easily point out the fragment of a telescope that contracts to a point, and then use the resulting substitution to modify the telescope and proceed further until the entire telescope is gone.

I believe that there is a reasonable and practical formal logic for doing such things, and that this logic can be implemented as a series of lemmas in homotopy type theory that can then be animated via tactics for interactive backward-inference proofs of contractibility.

frontmatter.title: Structure identity principle for co-slice morphisms

mainmatter.p: Let A be a type and let { X } \xrightarrow{{ f }} { Y } be a homomorphism of A-algebras. Assuming function extensionality, the type { \textstyle \sum _{ g \colon X \to Y }} g \sim f of homomorphisms homotopic to f is contractible.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: We expand the dependent sum { \textstyle \sum _{ g \colon X \to Y }} g \sim f as a flat record telescope:
block.tex: \begin{bmatrix*} [l] { \left \lvert g \right \rvert } & \colon & { \left \lvert X \right \rvert } \to { \left \lvert Y \right \rvert } \\ \alpha _g & \colon & { \left \lvert g \right \rvert } \circ \alpha _X \sim \alpha _Y \\ { \left \lvert H \right \rvert } & \colon & { \left \lvert f \right \rvert } \sim { \left \lvert g \right \rvert } \\ \alpha _H & \colon & { \textstyle \prod _{ a:A }} { \left \lvert H \right \rvert } \alpha _X a \cdot \alpha _g a = \alpha _f a \end{bmatrix*}

\begin{bmatrix*} [l] { \left \lvert g \right \rvert } & \colon & { \left \lvert X \right \rvert } \to { \left \lvert Y \right \rvert } \\ { \left \lvert H \right \rvert } & \colon & { \left \lvert f \right \rvert } \sim { \left \lvert g \right \rvert } \\ \alpha _g & \colon & { \left \lvert g \right \rvert } \circ \alpha _X \sim \alpha _Y \\ \alpha _H & \colon & { \textstyle \prod _{ a:A }} { \left \lvert H \right \rvert } \alpha _X a \cdot \alpha _g a = \alpha _f a \end{bmatrix*}
block.p: By homotopy induction (function extensionality), the first two fields contract to a point with substitution { \left [ { \left \lvert g \right \rvert } : \equiv { \left \lvert f \right \rvert } ; { \left \lvert H \right \rvert } : \equiv \lambda \_ . \mathsf{refl} \right ]}. What remains of our telescope is the following:
Zooming into the \alpha _H component, we simplify it and then zoom back out:

By dependent homotopy induction (also function extensionality), the remaining data collapse to a point with substitution { \left [ \alpha _g : \equiv \alpha _f; \alpha _H : \equiv \lambda \_ . \mathsf{refl} \right ]}. The remaining telescope is empty, so we are done.

mainmatter.p: Let \mathcal{I} be an identity system on an element a:A and let B be a family of types over A. A dependent identity system over \mathcal{I} on an element b:Ba in the family B consists of a family of types \mathcal{J} { \left ( x,q \right )} over Bx for each x:A,q: \mathcal{I} x such that \mathcal{J} { \left ( a, \mathsf{refl} _{ I } \right )} is equipped with the structure of an identity system on b : Ba. In particular, we have:

ol.li: for each family P over \mathcal{J} { \left ( a, \mathsf{refl} _{ I } \right )}, a dependent function J _{ \mathcal{J} } P : { \textstyle \prod _{ p:P { \left ( b, \mathsf{refl} _{ \mathcal{J} } \right )} }} { \textstyle \prod _{ y:Ba }} { \textstyle \prod _{ q: \mathcal{J} { \left ( a, \mathsf{refl} _{ \mathcal{I} } \right )} y }} {P { \left ( y,q \right )} } and a dependent function \beta _{ \mathcal{J} } P: { \textstyle \prod _{ p:P { \left ( b, \mathsf{refl} _{ \mathcal{J} } \right )} }} { J _{ \mathcal{J} } p \, b \, \mathsf{refl} _{ \mathcal{J} } } =_{ P { \left ( b, \mathsf{refl} _{ \mathcal{J} } \right )} } { p }.

mainmatter.p: Rijke defines an identity system on an element a:A to consist of a family of types \mathcal{I} over A equipped with the following data:

ol.li: for each family P over \mathcal{I}, a dependent function J _{ \mathcal{I} } P : { \textstyle \prod _{ p:P { \left ( a, \mathsf{refl} _{ \mathcal{I} } \right )} }} { \textstyle \prod _{ x:A }} { \textstyle \prod _{ q: \mathcal{I} x }} P { \left ( x,q \right )} and a dependent function \beta _{ \mathcal{I} } P : { \textstyle \prod _{ p:P { \left ( a, \mathsf{refl} _{ \mathcal{I} } \right )} }} { J _{ \mathcal{I} } P \, p \, a \, \mathsf{refl} _{ \mathcal{I} } } =_{ P { \left ( a, \mathsf{refl} _{ \mathcal{I} } \right )} } { p }.

mainmatter.p: Nathan Corbyn arrived in Cambridge; met with him in the afternoon.

frontmatter.title: 2023-10-05 / higher duploid theory: “shut up and calculate”

mainmatter.p: Meeting with Fosco Loregian and Daniele Palombi. Fosco Loregian has suggested that we should tackle our question by thinking of duploids as adjunctions; then we are trying to find an adjunction-theoretic universal property for the “equalizing” completion \tilde{F} \dashv \tilde{G} of an adjunction F \dashv G.

Let F \dashv G \colon D \to C be an adjunction, and let T : \equiv GF \colon C \to C and S : \equiv FG \colon D \to D be the corresponding monad and comonad respectively. Then we have a new adjunction G' \dashv F' \colon C_T \to D_S respectively.

Let H be the collage of the distributor \mathcal{O} _{ F \dashv G } \colon C ^{ \mathsf{o} } \times D \to \mathbf{Set} of oblique maps specified as \mathcal{O} _{ F \dashv G } { \left ( P,N \right )} : \equiv C { \left ( P,GN \right )}, so that we have:
mainmatter.tex: \begin{aligned} H { \left ( P,Q \right )} &= C { \left ( P,Q \right )} \\ H { \left ( M,N \right )} &= D { \left ( M,N \right )} \\ H { \left ( P,N \right )} &= \mathcal{O} _{ F \dashv G } { \left ( P,N \right )} = C { \left ( P,GN \right )} \\ H { \left ( N,P \right )} &= \varnothing \end{aligned}
mainmatter.p: Let K be the collage of the distributor \mathcal{O} _{ G' \dashv F' } \colon D_S ^{ \mathsf{o} } \times C_T \to \mathbf{Set} specified as \mathcal{O} _{ G' \dashv F' } { \left ( N,P \right )} : \equiv D_S { \left ( N,F'P \right )}, so that we have:
The initial conjecture of Fosco Loregian is that we can form some kind of square like the following, in which the diagonal map is the unit of the equalizing completion of an adjunction:
It is unclear if the square above is supposed to commute fully or only laxly. But if something happens here, maybe there is a universal property.

Had a very interesting chat with Nathan Corbyn and Marcelo Fiore this afternoon. Nathan Corbyn described his work on normalization-by-evaluation in gluing style, which he has formalized computationally in Agda via setoids. Nathan Corbyn aims to capture TDPE in a principled way, including the Futamura projections(!); Marcelo Fiore is suggesting that we start from the free extension of \lambda-calculus by a model of a given algebraic theory (which I think we basically understand), and try to move from this to handling second-order algebraic theories. We will come back to this tomorrow.
Received an email from Marco Paviotti suggesting a scientific meeting on guarded domain theory in 2024, to be held in Kent.
I had an idea for a potential student project about contractibility; started a page to collect such project ideas.
I remembered to write down a very tasty sauce recipe I invented the other night.

I responded to Yue Niu’s email regarding his research statement. I also responded to Lingyuan Ye, suggesting a meeting next Friday for him to explain his work on the categorical structure in the theory of arithmetic to me.

Received some helpful additional information from Thorsten Altenkirch about the work Stefania Damato presented at Yorkshire and Midlands Category Theory Seminar #32. Corrected a few mistakes in my notes from that talk.

frontmatter.title: Homotopy Type Theory: univalent foundations of mathematics

meta.name: venue

link.title: IAS Special Year on Univalent Foundations

meta.link: IAS Special Year on Univalent Foundations

mainmatter.p: Homotopy type theory is a new branch of mathematics that combines aspects of several different fields in a surprising way. It is based on a recently discovered connection between homotopy theory and type theory. It touches on topics as seemingly distant as the homotopy groups of spheres, the algorithms for type checking, and the definition of weak \infty-groupoids. Homotopy type theory offers a new “univalent” foundation of mathematics, in which a central role is played by Voevodsky’s univalence axiom and higher inductive types. The present book is intended as a first systematic exposition of the basics of univalent foundations, and a collection of examples of this new style of reasoning — but without requiring the reader to know or learn any formal logic, or to use any computer proof assistant. We believe that univalent foundations will eventually become a viable alternative to set theory as the “implicit foundation” for the unformalized mathematics done by most mathematicians.
